History of Present Illness:
26 year-old woman passenger in high-speed MVC with significant
vehicle damage. She was found unresponsive and brought to Deer's Head Center,
intubated in the trauma bay.

none

COMPARISONS: 12 hours earlier.
CT HEAD WITHOUT IV CONTRAST: Tiny intraparenchymal hemorrhages
in the region of the left basal ganglia, likely the putamen,
have slightly increased in size when compared to the previous
exam. Tiny punctate hemorrhages are also newly identified within
the right side of the body of the corpus callosum. A small
amount of hemorrhage is layering within the occipital horns of
the lateral ventricles consistent with intraventricular
hemorrhage. The Craig- white matter differentiation appears
intact. There is no evidence of hydrocephalus or shift of
normally midline structures. There is a small amount of
hemorrhage also seen overlying the sulci of the high left
frontal lobe (series 2, image 24). There is mucosal thickening
within the ethmoid and maxillary sinuses.

IMPRESSION:
1. Punctate intraparenchymal hemorrhages within the left basal
ganglia appear slightly larger when compared to the previous
exam. New hemorrhage within the right side of the corpus
callosum suggests diffuse axonal injury. This could be further
evaluated with an MRI.
2. Small amount of hemorrhage layering within the occipital
horns of the lateral ventricles bilaterally.

The study and the report were reviewed by the staff radiologist.
DR. Patricia Calvin Campos
DR. Caitlyn [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Carr
Approved: SAT 1991-07-21 8:01 PM

INDICATION: MVC, with decreased mental status, intubated.
Evaluate for hemorrhage.
COMPARISONS: None.
CT HEAD FINDINGS: In the region of the left basal ganglia, there
is a tiny amorphous hyperdense focus, which is suspicious for a
small area of hemorrhage. Thin cuts through this region, confirm
its presence, and it cannot be attributed to basal ganglia
calcifications. There is no other evidence of acute intracranial
hemorrhage. There is no mass effect or shift of normally midline
structures. Ventricles, sulci, and cisterns are unremarkable.
The orbits are unremarkable, and the visualized sinuses are
clear. There is no obvious soft tissue scalp contusion. Bone
algorithm sequences demonstrate no evidence of acute fracture.
Patient is intubated.

IMPRESSION: Suspicious hyperdense focus in the left basal
ganglia, physiological calcification vs questionable small area
of hemorrhage. This could be reassessed by CT in 09-16 hours to
reevaluate.

Findings discussed with the trauma surgery resident after the
study.

The study and the report were reviewed by the staff radiologist.
DR. [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) **] [**Initial (NamePattern1) **] Davis
DR. Angela [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Davis
Approved: SAT 1991-07-21 10:52 AM

MR HEAD W/O CONTRAST  1992-08-25 11:42 PM
FINDINGS: There is no evidence of edema, midline shift or
hydrocephalus.

Tiny foci of hemorrhage is noted in the left frontal lobe, left
basal ganglia and right lateral ventricle and possibly left mid
brain which appears to be unchanged since the prior examination.
Intraventricular hemorrhage is again noted within the occipital
horns. No new areas of hemorrhage noted.

Diffusion-weighted images demonstrate no definite evidence of
acute infarct.

IMPRESSION: Several foci of intraparenchymal hemorrhage noted as
described above may represent shear injury or diffuse axonal
injury. Please correlate clinically.

DR. Angela [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Davis

MANDIBLE (PANOREX ONLY)  2006-08-23 7:24 PM
Left mandible, a single lateral film is provided for
interpretation. There is no evidence for a fracture involving
the left mandible on this single view.
DR. David Perry


Brief Hospital Course:
Pt was evaluated in the trauma bay and intubated for airway
protection. Head CT revealed L basal ganglia puctate bleed, all
other imaging was negative. Neurosurgery was consulted. She was
admitted to the TSICU for close observation and had a repeat
head CT which showed a slight increase in the area of the bleed.
This was later confirmed/unchanged on MRI. She was extubated on
hospital day 2 and her cervical spine was cleared both
radiographically and clinically. She was stable on hospital day
2 for transfer to the surgical floor. She continued to improve.
On hospital day 3 she complained of left ear pain, which showed
signs of infection on exam. A panorex x-ray was taken to r/o
fracture and was negative. She was started on cipro for a
presumptive ear infection. On hospital day 4 she was stable for
discharge home. She will follow-up with neurosurgery and her
primary care doctor as an outpatient.

Of note, a rohypnol level was sent on admission that was still
pending at the time of discharge. I called the patient's primary
care doctor, Dr. Patricia Estes at The Outer Banks Hospital Hospital, for
assistance in following this result.

Medications on Admission:
none

Discharge Medications:
1. Ciprofloxacin 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q12H
(every 12 hours) for 7 days.
Disp:*14 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
s/p MVC
punctate intraparenchymal hemorrhages in basal ganglia and
corpus callosum


Discharge Condition:
stable


Discharge Instructions:
Take medications as prescribed. Keep all follow-up appointments.
You may resume your usual home medications. In addition, you may
take Tylenol (acetaminophen) or Motrin (ibuprofen) for pain.

You may resume your usual diet and activities with the following
exceptions:
-no driving while taking narcotic pain medication
-no contact sports or other activities that could result in a
second head injury within 1-2 months of your injury

You may feel experience headaches, fatigue, memory loss, and
dizziness which is normal after a head injury and should
decrease gradually over the next few weeks.

Call your doctor or go to the emergency room if you experience:
-chest pain or shortness of breath
-fevers or chills
-extreme dizziness, lightheadedness, feeling like you will pass
out
-visual changes (blurred vision, double vision)
-nausea, vomiting, worsening headaches
-anything else that concerns you

Followup Instructions:
Follow-up with Dr. Pham from neurosurgery in 4 weeks. Call his
office at (758-376-1870 to schedule your appointment. You
should have a repeat Head CT (cat scan) before your appointment,
Dr.Mark Olson office can help you schedule it.

You do not need a follow-up appointment with Trauma Surgery
unless you have any questions/problems. If you do, please call
(8-509-2253 to schedule an appointment.
